1: אַשְׁרֵי מַשְׂכִּיל אֶל-דָּל (ʾashrê maskîl ʾel-dāl, "Blessed is the one who cares for the vulnerable") — dāl: thin, weak, poor — the one who is diminished (from dālal: to dangle, to be brought low). This is the physically and socially vulnerable person, not the spiritually poor (ʾānî). Maskîl here is not the psalm-type but the verb: "who acts wisely/attentively toward" the vulnerable. The wisdom of the caring person is the reciprocating structure: the LORD will deliver that person in the day of trouble — care given produces care received.
2: יְהוָה יִשְׁמְרֵהוּ וִיחַיֵּהוּ (YHWH yishmĕrêhû wîḥayyêhû, "The LORD protects and preserves his life") — shāmar: to keep, to guard; ḥāyāh (piel: to keep alive, to give life). אַשַּׁר בָּאָרֶץ (ʾushshar bāʾāreṣ, "he is regarded as blessed in the land") — ʾāshar (pual: to be called/regarded as blessed) — a passive form of the ʾashrê root. The social recognition of blessedness follows from the divine protection. וְאַל-תִּתְּנֵהוּ בְּנֶפֶשׁ אֹיְבָיו (wĕʾal-tittĕnêhû bĕnepesh ʾōyĕbāyw, "you will not hand him over to his enemies' desire") — nepesh (soul/desire): the enemies' deep appetite for his destruction is thwarted.
3: יְהוָה יִסְעָדֶנּוּ עַל-עֶרֶשׂ דְּוָי (YHWH yisʿādennû ʿal-ʿereś dĕwāy, "The LORD supports him on his bed of illness") — sāʿad: to support, to sustain (as a crutch or prop — the word used for bread "sustaining the heart" in Gen 18:5). ʿEreś: a couch, bed; dĕwāy: illness, languishing (from dāwāh: to be faint, sick — the same root as the "sick with love" in Song 2:5). כָּל-מִשְׁכָּבוֹ הָפַכְתָּ בְחָלְיוֹ (kol-mishkābô hāpaktā bĕḥolyô, "in his sickness you bring him back to health") — literally "you have turned/transformed all his bed in his illness" — the bed of illness reversed.
4: אֲנִי-אָמַרְתִּי יְהוָה חָנֵּנִי (ʾănî-ʾāmartî YHWH ḥonnênî, "As for me, I said, 'LORD, have mercy on me'") — the shift to the Psalmist's own cry: ḥānan: to be gracious, to show unearned favor. רְפָאָה נַפְשִׁי כִּי-חָטָאתִי לָךְ (rĕpāʾāh napshî kî-ḥāṭāʾtî lāk, "heal my soul, for I have sinned against you") — rāpāʾ: to heal; nāpesh: the whole animated self. The confession that the sickness may be connected to sin — not as a theological claim that all sickness is punishment, but as the Psalmist's personal acknowledgment of a possible link. The prayer is for healing of the nāpesh (the inner person), not merely the body.
5: אוֹיְבַי יֹאמְרוּ רַע לִי (ʾôyĕbay yōʾmĕrû raʿ lî, "My enemies speak wickedly against me") — literally "speak evil of me/to me." מָתַי יָמוּת וְאָבַד שְׁמוֹ (mātay yāmût wĕʾābad shĕmô, "When will he die and his name vanish?") — māwet: death; ʾābad: to perish, to be destroyed. The enemies' desire: not merely his death but the obliteration of his shēm (name/reputation) — the erasure of his social existence. In the ancient world, the continuity of one's name was the nearest equivalent to immortality.
6: וְאִם-בָּא לִרְאוֹת שָׁוְא יְדַבֵּר (wĕʾim-bāʾ lirʾôt shāwľ yĕdabbêr, "When one of them comes to visit me, he mouths empty words") — shāwľ: emptiness, worthlessness, vanity — the visit is a social performance concealing hostile intelligence-gathering. לִבּוֹ יִקְבָּץ-אָוֶן לוֹ (libbô yiqbaṣ-ʾāwen lô, "while inwardly filing away material against me") — qābaṣ: to gather, to collect; ʾāwen: trouble, iniquity, harmful intention. The heart as the storage chamber for accumulated ammunition against the sick person.
7: יַחַד עָלַי יִתְלַחֲשׁוּ כָּל-שֹׂנְאָי (yaḥad ʿālay yitlaḥăshû kol-śōnĕʾāy, "All who hate me whisper together against me") — lāḥash: to whisper, to mutter (originally of the snake's hiss — the same root used for the whisperer/serpent of Gen 3:1). Yaḥad: together, as a unified conspiracy. עָלַי יַחְשְׁבוּ רָעָה לִי (ʿālay yaḥshĕbû rāʿāh lî, "they are plotting disaster for me") — ḥāshab: to think, to devise, to calculate (the accounting word — planning evil as deliberate design).
8: דְּבַר-בְּלִיַּעַל יָצוּק בּוֹ (dĕbar-bĕliyaʿal yāṣûq bô, "Something incurable has seized him") — bĕliyaʿal: the chaos/worthlessness force (as in Ps 18:4 — Belial). The phrase "a word/thing of Belial is poured into him" — the illness as the hostile force of chaotic destruction fastened on him. וַאֲשֶׁר שָׁכַב לֹא-יוֹסִיף לָקוּם (waʾăsher shākab lōʾ-yôsîp lāqûm, "he will never get up from where he is lying") — the enemies' confident prediction: this is terminal. The language of the lying-down of death.
9: גַּם-אִישׁ שְׁלוֹמִי אֲשֶׁר-בָּטַחְתִּי בוֹ (gam-ʾîsh shĕlômî ʾăsher-bāṭaḥtî bô, "Even my closest friend, whom I trusted") — ʾîsh shĕlômî: "man of my peace/wholeness" — the one with whom one shares shālôm (covenant wholeness). Bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on. אוֹכֵל לַחְמִי הִגְדִּיל עָלַי עָקֵב (ʾōkêl laḥmî higdîl ʿālay ʿāqêb, "who shared my bread, has turned against me") — ʾōkêl laḥmî: "one who eats my bread" — table-fellowship as the covenant bond; bread-sharing was the sign of covenant relationship (Gen 31:54; Exod 18:12). ʿĀqêb: heel — "lifted up the heel against me." Jesus applies this verse to Judas in John 13:18 ("the one who eats my bread has turned against me"), making the betrayal the fulfillment of this pattern.
10: וְאַתָּה יְהוָה חָנֵּנִי וַהֲקִימֵנִי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ḥonnênî wahăqîmênî, "But you, LORD, have mercy on me and restore me") — the turn from the enemies' confident prediction (he will never rise) to the prayer for rising. Qûm (hiphil: to cause to rise, to set upright): the counterword to the lying-down of v8. וַאֲשַׁלְּמָה-לָּהֶם (waʾăshallĕmāh-lāhem, "so that I may give them what they deserve") — shālam (piel: to repay, to complete — the same root as shālôm). The imprecation: repayment in full to those who violated the covenant of peace.
11: בְּזֹאת יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-חָפַצְתָּ בִּי (bĕzōʾt yādaʿtî kî-ḥāpaṣtā bî, "By this I know that you are pleased with me") — ḥāpēṣ: to delight in, to take pleasure in (the covenant-pleasure word of Ps 18:19; 5:4). The sign of divine pleasure: כִּי לֹא-יָרִיעַ אֹיְבִי עָלָי (kî lōʾ-yārîaʿ ʾōyĕbî ʿālay, "my enemy has not triumphed over me") — rûaʿ (hiphil: to shout in triumph, to raise the victory cry). The enemy's silence is read as the sign of God's favorable attention.
12: וַאֲנִי בְּתֻמִּי תָּמַכְתָּ בִּי (waʾănî bĕtummî tāmaktā bî, "As for me, you hold me up in my integrity") — tōm: integrity, completeness, blamelessness (from tāmam: to be complete — the root of tāmîm). Tāmak: to hold, to support. וַתַּצִּיבֵנִי לְפָנֶיךָ לְעוֹלָם (wattaṣṣîbênî lĕpānêkā lĕʿôlām, "and place me before your face forever") — yāṣab (hiphil: to set upright, to station) — permanently positioned before the divine face, the place of covenant nearness and honor.
13: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵהָעוֹלָם וְעַד הָעוֹלָם (bārûk YHWH ʾĕlōhê yisrāʾêl mêhāʿôlām wĕʿad hāʿôlām, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting") — this verse is the closing doxology of Book I of the Psalter (Ps 1-41), not part of Psalm 41 itself. Each of the five books of the Psalter closes with a similar doxological formula (see also Ps 72:18-19; 89:52; 106:48; 150:6). Mêhāʿôlām wĕʿad hāʿôlām: "from the age/hidden-one to the age/hidden-one" — the vast expanse of time in both directions, enclosing all temporal existence. אָמֵן וְאָמֵן (ʾāmên wĕʾāmên, "Amen and Amen") — the double liturgical affirmation: ʾāmên (truly so, let it stand firm — from ʾāman: to be firm, reliable, faithful). The congregation's ratification of the doxology.
